ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for corn and cheese croquette; SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dish variants at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising 2–3 deep-fried corn and cheese croquettes served with a jammy soft-boiled egg, grated parmesan, and a fresh frisée herb salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Croquette, potato-based, fried; supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for corn and cheese croquette exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for potato croquette (fried, breaded) and corn-based fried snacks, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for cheese-containing fried dishes. Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with the adapted USDA/HPB composite estimate. Energy 250 kcal/100g, protein 8.3g, fat 18.3g, carbohydrate 13.3g are plausible for a deep-fried cheese-filled croquette and are within the range of USDA fried croquette analogues.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato croquette / breaded fried potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for corn and cheese croquette. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato croquette / breaded fried potato product (GI ~72, high category). Corn contributes additional rapidly digestible starch; the breadcrumb coating is refined wheat. The composite GI of 72 is a defensible upper-range estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 13.33g/100g × 300g serving) / 100 = 72 × 40g / 100 ≈ 28.8, rounded to 32 to account for the corn starch contribution. GL = (72 × 44.4g available carb per 300g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g combined with high saturated fat 6.67 g/100g from parmesan, cream, and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and request a baked or air-fried preparation to reduce both sodium load and saturated fat intake, as parmesan cheese and deep-frying oil together push total sodium and arterial-stiffness risk above recommended thresholds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from potato-based croquette shell and refined breadcrumb coating, with low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100g) providing insufficient glycaemic modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high GI of approximately 72 and high glycemic load per serving (~32); if consumed, portion should not exceed one croquette (~80–100 g) and should be paired with high-fibre vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold; trans fat 0.17 g/100g is present; cholesterol 100 mg/100g from egg and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish as the combination of saturated fat above 6.6 g/100g, detectable trans fat at 0.17 g/100g, and cholesterol at 100 mg/100g collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; a baked version with reduced cheese would substantially lower the atherogenic fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat 0.17 g/100g is pro-inflammatory; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, trans fat at 0.17 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory, and potato as a nightshade may aggravate solanine-related joint symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Corn and Cheese Croquette is a Western-style deep-fried snack dish served in Singaporean cafés and casual dining restaurants. The croquettes are made from a filling of charred and creamed corn blended with cheese (typically parmesan), encased in a breadcrumb coating and deep-fried until golden and crispy. A standard plate comprises 2–3 croquettes (approximately 300 g total) served alongside a jammy soft-boiled egg, additional grated parmesan, and a fresh frisée herb salad. The exterior is crunchy and golden while the interior is soft, creamy, and savoury with a sweet corn flavour and sharp cheese notes. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 250 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 18.3 g/100g (of which 6.67 g is saturated fat), moderate protein at 8.3 g/100g, and relatively low carbohydrate at 13.3 g/100g. Sodium is 300 mg/100g, and trans fat is present at 0.17 g/100g from the deep-frying process. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), driven by the potato-starch and refined breadcrumb base, with a glycemic load of approximately 32 per standard serving. The dish is classified as vegetarian but is not vegan, halal, or gluten-free. It is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. Common allergens include gluten, egg, and dairy.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for corn and cheese croquette exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA fried croquette and corn-based fried snack analogues, cross-referenced with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Core macros are plausible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried potato croquette. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this specific corn and cheese croquette preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition database as of 2026-04-16; no approved source reports these values for this composite dish. Trans fat is reported at 0.17 g/100g from the input record, consistent with deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or commercial frying oils. Calcium (83.33 mg/100g), iron (1.0 mg/100g), cholesterol (100 mg/100g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg/100g) are retained from the input record and are plausible given the parmesan, egg, and corn content respectively. The dish is classified as vegetarian (contains egg and dairy) but is not vegan. Halal status is unconfirmed as the source restaurant and ingredient provenance are not verified. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for fried potato croquette; no direct GI measurement for corn and cheese croquette was found in any approved source. Ethnic tags 'Cake', 'Pasta', 'Burger' from the input record were removed as they are not applicable to this dish.
